F,q,rn Polrrrc.rr PnacrrcBs CouvrrssroN
P.O. Box 807 . 428 J Street . Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(et6) 322-s660 ' 5(i#Jf,i3igb'd,
Sukhi K. Sandhu, Esq.
Atkinson, Andelson, Loy a,
Ruud & Romo
17871Park Plaza Drive, Suite 200
Cerritos, CA 907 03 -8597
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-00-179
Dear Ms. Sandhu:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Jean Carpenter,l a
member of the Baldwin Park Unified School District's Governing Board, regarding her
duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("the Act").2
Since your request does not concern a specific pending decision, we decline to provide
formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(C) ) Instead, we are treating your
request as one for informal assistance under the Act. Informal assistance does not confer
the immunity provided by formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3).) Also, please
note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have
already taken place. Moreover, this informal assistance is limited to the provisions of the
Act. The Commission does not advise on other conflict of interest laws such as
Government Code section 1090.
QUESTIONS
1. 
Does school board member Jean Carpenter have a conflict of interest as to
any decision by the school board involving her employer, Kaiser Permanente ("Kaiser")?
2. 
Would abstention from any discussions and/or decisions involving Kaiser
be suffrcient to avoid violation of the Act's conflict of interest provisions?
' Your letter also included requests for advice on behalf of Ralph Nunez and Kallleen Gair, who
have since vacated their seats on the school board.
2 Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear attitle 2, sections
I 8 I 09- I 8996, of the California Code of Regulations.


Our File No. I-00-179
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. 
We are not able to answer this question because your request lacks facts of
a specific governmental decision that would enable us analyze the foreseeability of a
material financial effect.
2. 
Yes. Abstention from any discussions, negotiations and decisions related
to the school district's contract with Kaiser would prevent a violation of the conflict of
interest provisions of the Act.
FACTS
Jean Carpenter has been continuously employed by Kaiser snce 1977. She was
first elected to the Governing Board of the Baldwin Park Unified School District
("district") approximately five years ago. The district has a contract'ftith Kaiser,
pursuant to which Kaiser provides medical services to the district's employees.
The school board has not had to make any decisions regarding the district's
contract with Kaiser thus far during Ms. Carpenter's tenure on the board, but it is
anticipated that the board will have to address issues related to this contract at some time
in the future.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. In order to determine whether the
prohibition in section 87100 applies to a given decision, Regulation 18700 provides the
following eight-step analysis.
Step One: Is the individual a "public oflicial?"
As a member of the Governing Board of the Baldwin Park Unified School
District, Ms. Carpenter is a "member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency" and, therefore, is a "public offrcial" subject to the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act. (Section 82048; Regulation 18701(a).)
Step Two: Is the public official making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?
A public ofiicial "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her


Our File No. I-00-179
Page No. 3
position and without significant substantive review, the official negotiates, advises or
makes recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the governmental decision.
(Regulation 18702.2.) A public oflicial is attempting to use his or her official position to
influence a decision i{, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears
before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation
18702.3.) For purposes of the conflict of interest provisions of the Act, a public offrcial
can avoid a conflict by abstaining from making, participating in making, and influencing
a decision in which the offrcial has a financial interest.
Step Three: Does the public official have economic interests?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from
economic interests. The "economic interests" from which conflicts of interest may arise
are defined in Regulations 18703-18703.5. Identifying which, if any, of these economic
interests Ms. Carpenter has is the third step in ana\yziigwhether shl has a conflict of
interest under the Act. (See Regulation 18700(bX3) ) There are sixkinds of economic
interests:
A public official^has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect3 investment of $2,000 or more (Section szio:(a); Regulation
1 8703.1(a));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 871 03 (b); Regulation 18703 .2);
a
a
a
a
a
a
A public ofiicial has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 1 8703.3);
A public oflicial has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management
(Section 87103(d); Regulation I 8703. l(b));
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $320 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section
87103(e); Regulation 18706.9;
A public oflicial has an economic interest in his or her personal finances (expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities), as well as those of his or her immediate family.
(Section 87 103 ; Regulation I 8703. 5).
Your facts indicate that Ms. Carpenter is employed by and receives income from
Kaiser, which is presumably in excess of $500 per year. As noted above, this would
' An indirect investment or interest in real property means, among other things, any investment or interest
owned by the official's immediate family. (Section 87103.)


Our File No. I-00-179
Page No. 4
mean that Ms. Carpenter has an economic interest in Kaiser for purposes of the Act, as a
source of income and as a business entity by which she is employed.
Step Four: Are the public official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved
in the decision?
Because you are not asking about a specific governmental decision, we can only
discuss the following steps generally. A business entity that is a source of income to the
official is directly involved in a decision before an offrcial's agency when that entity,
either directly or by an agent: "(1) [i]nitiates the proceeding in which the decision will be
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; (2) [i]s a named party
in, or is the subject of the proceeding concerning the decision before the official or the
official's agency." A business entity is "the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves
the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with," the subject entrty. (Regulation 18704.1(a).) In all other
cases, the source of income is considered indirectly involved in the dtcision.
You have stated that the district has a contract with Kaiser for the provision of
medical services to the district's employees. Because Kaiser is a named party to, and is
the subject of any proceeding involving the contract, it is directly involved in any
decision concerning that contract.
Steps Five and Six Will the financial effect of the decision on the oflicial's economic
interest be material and reasonably foreseeable?
Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official
must evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on any of those economic interests. This determination takes two steps.
First, the ofiicial must find and apply the applicable materiality standard set forth in
Commissionregulations. (Regulation 18700(b)(5), Regulation 18705, et seq.) After
finding the applicable materiality standard, the offrcial must then decide whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the standard will be met. (Regulation 18700(b)(6) )
With limited exceptions, where sources of income are directly involved in a
decision (as discussed above), any reasonably foreseeable financial effect is deemed
material. (Regulations 18705. 1(a) and I 8705.3(a).)
Regulation 18706 provides that "[a] material financial effect on an economic
interest is reasonably foreseeable, within the meaning of Government Code section
87103, if it is substantially likely that one or more ofthe materiality standards (see Cal.
Code Regs ., tit. 2 $$ 18704, 18705) applicable to that economic interest will be met as a
result of the governmental decision." A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable. (Inre Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) Generally,
the foreseeability and materiality analysis must be applied on a decision-by-decision
basis. Since we do not have facts of a specific decision, we cannot provide any further
advice on foreseeability.


Our File No. I-00-179
Page No. 5
Steps Seven and Eight: Exceptions
We have not gone on to analyze the latter two steps. Step seven is an exception
that applies where the reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on the
oflicial's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
and step eight is an exception that applies when the offrcial is legally required to
participate in the decision. You have not provided any facts to suggest that these
exceptions may apply.
If you have any other questions regarding this matteq please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel
Acr
By:
B. Armstrong
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

any decision by the school board involving her employer, Kaiser Permanente ("Kaiser")?
2.

be suffrcient to avoid violation of the Act's conflict of interest provisions?
' Your letter also included requests for advice on behalf of Ralph Nunez and Kallleen Gair, who
have since vacated their seats on the school board.
2Our Page No. 2

CONCLUSION: 1. 
We are not able to answer this question because your request lacks facts of
